model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, the baseline LR is likely conservative for Adam on this compact CNN. Raising LR should improve early optimization progress; a slight WD reduction offsets potential underfitting during short training.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    total_steps = TRAIN_MAX_STEPS if TRAIN_MAX_STEPS is not None else steps_per_epoch * 100","old":"    total_steps = steps_per_epoch * 100","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The run is fixed to 256 optimizer steps, so decaying LR across exactly that horizon should improve late-step convergence versus a constant LR. This keeps complexity minimal while aligning scheduler length with the actual budget.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Cosine decay over only 256 steps regressed, so revert to constant LR and push a slightly higher step size to improve early progress under the fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Increase early feature capacity with a modest width bump while returning to the previously better LR. This tests whether undercapacity, rather than step size, is limiting fixed-budget performance.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size lets each optimizer step consume more unique examples, often improving validation loss in this short-horizon regime without adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strong gain from 64 to 128 suggests the fixed-step regime is data-exposure limited. Increasing to 256 should further raise unique examples seen within 256 updates, potentially improving validation loss again.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch size 256, gradient noise is lower than earlier settings; a moderately higher LR can improve optimization speed within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"With high batch and LR, decoupled weight decay can stabilize updates and improve generalization versus Adam’s coupled L2 behavior at the same hyperparameters.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"AdamW underperformed at this setting; reverting to Adam restores the better optimizer behavior before exploring further changes.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Current bests came from increasing batch under fixed steps. Moving to 384 further increases sample coverage per update and may reduce validation loss again in this budget-limited regime.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Performance keeps improving as batch size increases under fixed 256 steps, indicating data exposure remains the dominant bottleneck. Testing 512 is the next minimal extension of that trend.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With batch size now at 512, optimization can usually tolerate a larger step size. A moderate LR increase may improve progress within the same fixed 256 updates.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Improvements have been consistently driven by larger batch size under fixed 256 updates, so increasing to 768 is a direct continuation of the strongest observed trend for better sample coverage per step.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size 768 regressed substantially, so reverting to 512 returns to the strongest known data-exposure setting before testing further refinements.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"At batch 512 the model now sees multiple effective epochs within 256 steps, so slightly stronger regularization may reduce overfitting and improve validation loss.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1.5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Regularization strength improved loss at 1e-4; a modest additional increase tests whether this high-data-exposure regime benefits from slightly stronger decay.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1.5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The increase to 1.5e-4 slightly regressed versus 1e-4, so reverting to the better regularization level restores the strongest known setting before final tuning.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Current configuration is stable and near-best; a small upward LR sweep is the highest-leverage remaining scalar test for faster convergence within fixed 256 steps.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"3e-3 overshot badly; move back near the best region with a slightly lower-than-2.5e-3 value to test if a small reduction improves stability and validation loss.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The 2.4e-3 trial regressed; restore the best-performing learning rate setting for the final step.","secondary_modes":["caching","micro"]}
